name: Oklahoma Office of Management & Enterprise Services (OMES)
tagline: Oklahoma's central back office: OMES runs the state's consolidated IT, owns Central Purchasing and the statewide contracts most public buyers use, and manages the state budget and finances — the single front door for selling technology to Oklahoma state government.
overview: The Office of Management and Enterprise Services (OMES) is Oklahoma's central administrative agency, combining three functions that matter to a technology seller in one place: consolidated statewide IT (via OMES Information Services), Central Purchasing (the state's procurement authority and statewide-contract vehicle), and budget/finance for the executive branch. Under the Information Technology Consolidation and Coordination Act (62 O.S. §§35.1–35.9), OMES delivers mandatory shared IT services — email, networking, applications, cybersecurity — across executive-branch agencies and charges costs back on a cost-recovery rate model rather than running purely on a large general-revenue line. OMES Information Services supports 125+ agencies, boards and commissions (and the state CISO's security mission spans 180+ entities), which makes OMES both a large IT operator and the procurement path most Oklahoma public buyers travel. For a Dell SLG seller, OMES is best understood as the consolidated buying center and contract gateway for state-government technology in Oklahoma.

budget.intro: OMES is funded by a mix of direct appropriations and cost-recovery chargebacks: the IT Consolidation Act requires OMES to provide mandatory shared services and bill agencies back to recoup costs, so much of its IT spend flows through a service rate model rather than a single appropriation. Specific appropriation figures are set each session by the Legislature; treat the numbers below as directional and confirm against the current enacted budget.

stats.label: IT funding model
stats.note: Mandatory shared services billed back via a service rate model (62 O.S. §§35.1–35.9)

notes: OMES IT runs largely on chargebacks/cost recovery, not a single large GR line — the rate model, not one appropriation, drives most technology spend.

FY2027 appropriations include targeted items such as funding to comply with HB 3415 (a Central Purchasing database of state agency contracts with private vendors); confirm current enacted figures against the OMES FY27 Executive Budget and the Senate FY27 OMES Budget Performance Review.
SB68 lets some agencies hire IT staff directly, a softening of strict consolidation — watch how it shifts who owns a given IT buy.

initiatives.name: IT Strategic Plan (FY2026–2028)
initiatives.summary: CIO Dan Cronin's plan (announced Aug 29, 2025) is anchored on four goals: customer centricity, reducing complexity, workforce development, and strategic modernization — prioritizing secure, cloud-native infrastructure and shared platforms that cut duplication. The roadmap for where statewide IT investment is heading.

initiatives.name: Statewide AI strategy under first Chief AI Officer
initiatives.summary: Oklahoma named Tai Phan its first Chief AI & Technology Officer (announced Nov 21, 2025), fulfilling a Governor's Task Force on Emerging Technologies recommendation. He sets AI strategy, guardrails and ethics, and helps agencies find where AI streamlines operations — a buying center for responsible-AI and the infrastructure under it.

initiatives.name: Cloud-native modernization & legacy replacement
initiatives.summary: The strategic plan champions cloud-native infrastructure, shared identity management, and replacing legacy systems with real-time monitoring. Oklahoma is a public Google Cloud reference customer — frame infrastructure plays for a hybrid/cloud-forward environment.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity via Oklahoma Cyber Command
initiatives.summary: OMES Information Services runs Oklahoma Cyber Command, a 24/7/365 Security Operations Center protecting state users, devices, networks and data. New CISO Daniel Langley (started Oct 2025) oversees Cyber Command and security policy across 180+ entities — a focus on agency collaboration and closing vulnerabilities.

procurement.intro: Selling to Oklahoma state government runs through OMES Central Purchasing, the procurement authority under the Oklahoma Central Purchasing Act. The fastest path is a statewide contract — pre-competed and open to agencies and local entities — so confirm Dell's statewide-contract coverage and category codes before pursuing one-off solicitations.

vehicles.name: OMES Statewide Contracts
vehicles.detail: Pre-competed contracts open to state agencies, municipalities, higher education, school districts and other local governmental units — the primary buying vehicle. Searchable via OMES Central Purchasing's modernized statewide contract search.

vehicles.name: Central Purchasing solicitations
vehicles.detail: OMES Central Purchasing runs and oversees agency procurement under the Central Purchasing Act; larger or non-contract buys flow through its solicitations and oversight process.

vehicles.name: OMES Information Services shared services
vehicles.detail: Consolidated IT delivered by OMES and billed back via the service rate model — a route for infrastructure, networking, security and managed services across consolidated agencies.

powerStructure.name: Mark Wood
powerStructure.title: OMES Director
powerStructure.note: Appointed by Gov. Stitt effective Dec 17, 2025 (former Oklahoma Tax Commission chairman; 37+ yrs public accounting). Leads OMES across IT, procurement and budget — the top relationship for the account.

powerStructure.name: Dan Cronin
powerStructure.title: State Chief Information Officer
powerStructure.note: Appointed Jan 2025 (ex-Marriott global VP of engineering); author of the FY26–28 IT Strategic Plan and named to Constellation Research's 2026 Business Transformation 150. Owns statewide tech direction.

powerStructure.name: Tai Phan
powerStructure.title: Chief AI & Technology Officer
powerStructure.note: Oklahoma's first; announced Nov 21, 2025, selected by CIO Cronin. Sets AI strategy, guardrails and modernization delivery across 125+ agencies — the buying center for AI.

powerStructure.name: Daniel Langley
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer
powerStructure.note: Started Oct 2025 (ex-WaTech / Washington state). Leads statewide cyber strategy and Oklahoma Cyber Command's SOC across 180+ entities; reports under CIO Cronin.

powerStructure.name: OMES Central Purchasing
powerStructure.title: State procurement authority
powerStructure.note: Owns statewide contracts, solicitations and procurement oversight under the Central Purchasing Act — the path onto a sellable vehicle.

news.title: Gov. Stitt names Mark Wood OMES Director (effective Dec 17, 2025)
news.impact: New agency head over IT, procurement and budget — re-confirm the top relationship and any early priorities.

news.title: Tai Phan named Oklahoma's first Chief AI & Technology Officer
news.impact: Establishes a dedicated AI buying center and statewide AI guardrails — engage early on responsible-AI infrastructure.

news.title: Daniel Langley appointed State CISO over Oklahoma Cyber Command
news.impact: New security decision-maker across 180+ entities; rebuild the cybersecurity relationship map.

news.title: Oklahoma releases FY2026–2028 IT Strategic Plan
news.impact: Sets cloud-native modernization, complexity reduction and shared-platform priorities — align ISG and modernization plays to the four goals.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's OMES statewide-contract coverage and category codes — it's the access path to Oklahoma state agencies and many local buyers, not just OMES.
Map the new leadership bench early (Director Wood, CIO Cronin, AI Officer Phan, CISO Langley) — four key seats turned over between Oct and Dec 2025.
Lead modernization and AI-infrastructure plays tied to the FY26–28 IT Strategic Plan's cloud-native and shared-platform goals.
Engage Tai Phan's AI office on responsible-AI compute and data infrastructure as the statewide AI strategy and guardrails take shape.
Position security/infrastructure for Oklahoma Cyber Command's 24/7 SOC and CISO Langley's vulnerability-closing and agency-collaboration agenda.
